name: Tangun
description: Tangun is the legendary founder and god-king of Gojoseon, the first Korean kingdom. According to the Samguk Yusa and other historical records, Hwanung, the son of Hwanin (the Lord of Heaven), desired to descend to Earth and live among humans. Hwanin granted his wish, bestowing upon him three Heavenly Seals and dispatching him with 3,000 followers to Mount Taebaek (present-day Mount Myohyang). There, Hwanung established Sinsi, a divine city, and governed the world, teaching humans agriculture, medicine, arts, and laws. A bear and a tiger, desiring to become human, prayed to Hwanung. He gave them sacred mugwort and garlic and instructed them to avoid sunlight for 100 days. The tiger failed, but the bear persevered and transformed into a woman named Ungnyeo. Ungnyeo prayed to conceive a child, and Hwanung, moved by her devotion, transformed himself and impregnated her. She gave birth to Tangun, who later founded Gojoseon and reigned as its first king, establishing the Korean people's ancestral lineage and their connection to the divine. Tangun's reign is said to have lasted for 1,500 years, after which he abdicated and became a mountain god.
subtitle: The Founder King of Gojoseon

keyMyths.title: The Descent of Hwanung
keyMyths.description: Hwanung, son of the Lord of Heaven, descends to Earth with 3,000 followers to civilize humanity. He establishes Sinsi on Mount Taebaek and teaches humans essential skills and knowledge.

keyMyths.title: The Bear and the Tiger
keyMyths.description: A bear and a tiger seek to become human through Hwanung's intervention. The bear succeeds, transforming into Ungnyeo, while the tiger fails to endure the trial.

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Tangun
keyMyths.description: Ungnyeo prays for a child, and Hwanung transforms and impregnates her, resulting in the birth of Tangun. Tangun becomes the founder and first king of Gojoseon.

sources.author: Iryeon
sources.description: A collection of Korean folklore, legends, and historical accounts, including the myth of Tangun's founding of Gojoseon.
sources.text: A collection of Korean folklore, legends, and historical accounts, including the myth of Tangun's founding of Gojoseon.
